text: We started the procedure with the anterior utility port to get a view of how the chest wall generally looks, given our concern for adhesions.
And here you can see there was quite thick adhesions and very vascular posteriorly, but also these were found inferiorly and down towards the diaphragm.
We then made our camera port posteriorly, although this was slightly more anterior than usual, followed by our middle port.
After dissecting some of the adhesions, we inserted an Alexis port, which through our procedures has allowed us to get better views and reduce drippage from fat and blood.
And then we moved to posterior view.
We then opened the sheath over the pulmonary artery where we could see that there was normal anatomy and we tried to then move that lymph node out of the way quite carefully.
Following this, we decided then to develop the anterior oblique fissure further, and this was done using the Govidian ligature.
We felt this was appropriate as the thin appeared thin with a grade one fissure.
We then developed the very small posterior fissure to allow better visualisation of the apical segmental branch to the lower lobe.
And here you can see quite clearly there the apical segment and the lingula coming off anteriorly.
Following extensive and painstaking division of adhesions, which were also quite vascular, we managed to get down to the feeding vessel of the descending thoracic aorta.
We were a bit reluctant to try and skeletonize that vessel given risk of catastrophic haemorrhage.
As you can see there's still some adhesions and we felt that to gain better exposure we felt we needed to divide those further.
Following that we were then able to pass our instruments around this branch and here we were just using a right angled Roberts to allow us to give enough space to pass a stapler through.
You can see now that we're passing the Covidian tri stapler tan 45 millimeters through that little space.
We're on a full crank here and we're restricted by putting it in much further by the diaphragm.
Once we were happy that we were definitely going to be across, Mr. Will then closed the stapler.
And after a few discussions in the background with Mr. Walker and some slight concern that we might leave a little bit intact, we then proceeded to staple across that branch.
On opening of the stapler, you can see there's a small strand and that seemed to be a bit of tension, so we immediately dropped the lobe and that little branch was divided with scissors.
And then we just wanted to make sure that we would be able to get a vascular clamp across there if required.
Following that, we proceeded just in a standard fashion to perform a left lower lobectomy.

We were just dissecting here with a straight Roberts to see if we could get under the segmental branch but following this we decided it would be easier and also cheaper to divide the artery including the segmental branch in one go.
using a Tan 30mm stapler.
Following division of the artery, they then proceeded to dissect out the lower lobe vein and were able to do this quite easily and divided the structure again with a tanned 30mm stapler.
And following this, we then had good access to the lower lobe bronchus.
We weren't too concerned about that lymph node that was just on the lateral aspect.
However, after a couple of crushes, we were then happy enough to staple this with a purple 45.
Just for peace of mind at the end of the procedure Mr. Will felt we needed some tachycele involved and therefore attempted to put a small patch over this.

Unfortunately you see it's quite dry at the moment and therefore it didn't want to stick very well to start with however after being placed and by using a wet peanut we were able to get a satisfactory position for this
We're not certain whether this would actually stop any bleeding if it was to haemorrhage.
And after that and a washout of the pleural cavity, we inserted a chest drain with an extra hole in the side as it was inserted quite far into the chest.
And following this, we inserted our extra pleural catheter down to sympathetic chain.
